input.user_input: Give me an overview of how courts apply the business judgment rule to decisions made by corporate directors.

actual:
The business judgment rule is a foundational principle of corporate law that protects corporate directors from personal liability and prevents courts from second-guessing their business decisions. Originating primarily from Delaware corporate law, which heavily influences other jurisdictions, the rule serves as a standard of judicial review.

Here is an overview of how courts apply the business judgment rule to decisions made by corporate directors.

### 1. The Core Presumption
Courts apply the business judgment rule as a rebuttable presumption. When a board of directors makes a decision, courts presume that the directors acted:
*   On an informed basis (with due care).
*   In good faith.
*   In the honest belief that the action taken was in the best interests of the company.

If these conditions are met, a court will not substitute its own judgment for that of the directors, even if the decision turns out to be disastrous for the company. The rationale is that judges are not business experts, and corporations must be allowed to take calculated risks without directors fearing personal liability for every misstep.

### 2. Rebutting the Presumption
To survive a motion to dismiss and hold directors liable, a plaintiff (usually a shareholder) bears the burden of rebutting the business judgment rule. Courts will look at whether the plaintiff has pleaded facts showing that the directors breached one of their core fiduciary duties. The presumption is typically overcome if the plaintiff can demonstrate:
